sections.name: 🗞️ General
sections.when: Major industry/market headlines that anchor the day — pricing changes, regulatory moves, big partnerships, exec moves, product launches everyone covered, infra announcements. Items multiple outlets reported on or that set the day's narrative.
sections.shape: single short paragraph (3–5 sentences) that names the 2–3 biggest stories with inline `[Title](link)` cites. Prose, no bullets, no hero link. Reads like the lead column of a newsletter, not a list.

sections.name: 🔒 Security
sections.when: Anything security-shaped: CVEs and vulnerability disclosures, prompt-injection / jailbreak / data-exfiltration findings against LLMs, model-safety or misuse incidents, malicious packages or supply-chain attacks (npm/PyPI/HF), auth bypasses, credential leaks, breach reports, advisories from vendors or CERTs. Include defensive research (red-team writeups, novel attack techniques) even without a CVE.
sections.shape: bullet list — `- [Title](link) — <severity-or-mechanism + 8–15 word what-and-impact>`. Lead with the most severe / most actionable item. Inline a CVE id or affected product/version when present.

sections.name: 🚀 Release Notes
sections.when: Official versioned product/library/framework/SDK releases — vendor changelogs, named model releases (e.g. "Claude Code 1.3", "Next.js 16"), formal feature launches with version numbers or release branding. NOT incremental research model releases (those go to Research) and NOT cool indie projects (those go to Showcase).
sections.shape: bullet list — `- [Title](link) — <product/version> · <8–15 word what-changed>`. Sort newest/biggest first.

sections.name: ✨ Showcase
sections.when: Cool indie projects, novel demos, Show HN-style launches, single-purpose tools, repos worth bookmarking. Distinct from Release Notes (versioned official releases) and Research (papers/benchmarks). The bar: would a developer think "oh that's neat" and click through?

sections.name: 🧠 Research
sections.when: Papers, preprints, arXiv items, benchmarks, ablations, methodology studies, model releases with architectural novelty or reported scores, datasets, safety/alignment/interpretability work, empirical "we measured X" writeups, red-team findings.
sections.shape: bullet list — `- [Title](link) — <factual 10–20 word description with the number or mechanism that matters>`. Sort by research significance (new method/finding first, incremental results last). No hero link, no section-level paragraph.

extra_instructions: **Always include a dedicated `## 🧪 Research & Experiments` section** when any spec-, standards-, or experiment-flavored items appear — TC39 proposals, WHATWG/W3C drafts, CSSWG working-group posts, browser-engine experiments (Chrome Status, WebKit what's-new, MDN RFCs), performance studies, new Web API proposals, or formal compatibility reports.
Lead with the item's *stage* and *scope* (Stage 2 proposal, origin trial, shipping in Chrome 124, etc.) then the developer implication. Cite inline as `[Title](link)`. Cap at 3–5 items.
Skip the section entirely on days with no spec- or experiment-shaped items rather than padding it with framework release notes.

sections.name: ## 🚀 Releases
sections.when: Items from `Slack #eng-releases` (or sources whose name starts with `Slack #eng-releases`) — these are the bot-posted release notes for production deploys. Each item already has the version, fixes, and other-changes inline in the summary.
sections.shape: bullet list — `- **<version>** — <8–15 words: top-line of what shipped (lead fix or feature, link to ENG ticket if cited inline as [ENG-xxxx](link))>`. Sort newest first by published date.

sections.name: ## 🚨 Alerts
sections.when: Items from `Slack #eng-alerts` — production alerts, error spikes, on-call pages, infrastructure incidents. Each is short.
sections.shape: bullet list — `- [<short summary>](link) — <8–12 words: what fired and any visible disposition (resolved, still firing, on-call ack)>`.

sections.name: ## 🧵 Discussions
sections.when: Items from any Slack channel where the message has thread replies folded into the summary (you'll see `---` followed by `@user: <reply>` lines in the item summary). These are the meaty discussions worth surfacing — design proposals, debates, decisions.
sections.shape: bullet list — `- **<one-line topic>** in <#channel> — <12–25 words: what was being decided/debated and where it landed, citing the root message as [thread](link)>`. Sort by reply_count (longest threads first).
